coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence sensing of Cu2+ ion and imaging of fungal cell by ultra-small fluorescent carbon dots derived from Acacia concinna seeds
coredata.dc:creator: Bhamore, Jigna R.
Jha, Sanjay
Park, Tae Jung
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we explored the use of Acacia concinna seeds (shikakai) as a green precursor for the fabrication of ultra-small fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). The synthesized ultra-small fluorescent CDs sense only Cu2+ ion through strong chelation between Cu2+ ion and organic groups of CDs. As a result, highly selective fluorescence “turn-off” probe was developed for assay of Cu2+ ion, causing remarkable fluorescence intensity quenching, which has ability to sense Cu2+ ion even at 0.0043 μM (4.3 nM). Furthermore, the ultra-small fluorescent CDs act as biocompatible probes for multicolour imaging of fungal (Penicillium sp.) cells. The practical application of the developed sensor was demonstrated by assaying of Cu2+ ion in spiked water samples, which confirms their significant analytical and bioanalytical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultra-small fluorescent CDs
Acacia concinna seeds
Cu2+ ion
Fluorescence spectrometer
Penicillium sp. cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of ultra-small CDs using shikakai
Fluorescence detection of Cu2+ ion using ultra-small CDs derived from shikakai
Fluorescence “turn-off” detection of Cu2+ ion using ultra-small CDs
Analytical performance of ultra-small CDs for detection of Cu2+ ion
Application of ultra-small CDs for assaying of Cu2+ ion in water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of ultra-small fluorescent CDs using shikakai as a carbon precursor via microwave heating and applications in Cu2+ ion sensing and Penicillium sp. cell imaging.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra (λex/λem = 390/468 nm) of ultra-small CDs derived from shikakai via microwave heating at 800 W for 2 min (Inset: CDs image in day light and under UV lamp at 365 nm).
HR-TEM images of (a) ultra-small fluorescent CDs and (b) histography.

Fluorescence spectra of ultra-small CDs (λex/λem = 390/468 nm) with various metal (Hg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+and Cu2+, 500 μM). Inset: Image of corresponding solutions under UV light at 365 nm.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of ultra-mall CDs (λex/λem = 390/468 nm) with different concentrations of Cu2+ ion ranging from 0.01 to 500 μM. Inset: Image of corresponding solutions under UV light at 365 nm. (b) Calibration curve constructed between the values of I0/I and concentration of Cu2+ ion (0.01–10.0 μM).

Confocal microscopic images of Penicillium sp. (a) bright field without ultra-small CDs, and with ultra-small CDs at excitation wavelength of (b) 405 nm (blue), (c) 488 nm (green) and (d) 561 nm (red) using CDs as fluorescent probe. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Analytical performance of the present method with the other reported methods for the detection of Cu2+ ion.
Analysis of Cu2+ ion in spiked water samples using ultra-small CDs as a fluorescent probe (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Fluorescence sensing of Cu2+ ion and imaging of fungal cell by ultra-small fluorescent carbon dots derived from Acacia concinna seeds

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, S.V. National Institute of Technology, Surat 395007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Gujarat Agricultural Biotechnology Institute, Navsari Agricultural University, Surat 395007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Institute of Interdisciplinary Convergence Research, Research Institute of Halal Industrialization Technology, Chung-Ang University, 84 Heukseok-ro, Dongjak-gu, Seoul 06974, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute of Halal Industrialization Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from shikakai for fluorescence detection of Cu2+ ion and fungal cell imaging.

In this work, we explored the use of Acacia concinna seeds (shikakai) as a green precursor for the fabrication of ultra-small fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). The synthesized ultra-small fluorescent CDs sense only Cu2+ ion through strong chelation between Cu2+ ion and organic groups of CDs. As a result, highly selective fluorescence “turn-off” probe was developed for assay of Cu2+ ion, causing remarkable fluorescence intensity quenching, which has ability to sense Cu2+ ion even at 0.0043 μM (4.3 nM). Furthermore, the ultra-small fluorescent CDs act as biocompatible probes for multicolour imaging of fungal (Penicillium sp.) cells. The practical application of the developed sensor was demonstrated by assaying of Cu2+ ion in spiked water samples, which confirms their significant analytical and bioanalytical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper, an important transitional metal, is extensively used in various fields such as industry, agriculture and medical applications [1]. It exhibits good redox chemistry and strong binding ability with various bioactive molecules that underlie its key functions in various biopathways including signal transduction, energy generation, dioxygen transport and activation [2]. At higher concentrations, it shows potential toxic nature towards various bioactive molecules, causing several disorders in various organs (liver and kidney) and Wilson's disease [3,4]. It enters into food chain by various direct or indirect applications, and also settle and deposit at the bottom of rivers and lakes [5]. Importantly, World Health Organization (WHO) has set the maximum permissible limit in drinking water for Cu2+ ion at 1.0 mg L−1 [6]. In view of this, the detection of trace levels Cu2+ ion in various matrices is an essential and very imperative for monitoring of Cu2+ ion in various environmental samples. For this reason, several conventional analytical techniques have been applied to detect and to quantify trace levels if Cu2+ ion in various kinds of samples, including, infrared spectroscopy [1], inductively coupled plasma spectroscopy [7], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [8], atomic absorption spectroscopy [9] and anodic stripping voltammetry [10,11], respectively. Even though these methods have been effectively detected trace levels of Cu2+ ion, unfortunately they are either expensive or time-consuming, which paralyzes their wider applications in environmental analysis. Therefore, a rapid and facile analytical method is essentially required for assaying of Cu2+ ion in various samples with high sensitivity.

In recent years, great efforts have been devoted on the development of facile and highly selective fluorescent sensors for assaying of trace level bioactive metal ions in bio- and enviro- analytical sciences [12–14]. Generally, the development of fluorescence-based metal ions sensors involves the design and fabrication of active organic functional groups that have strong tendency to bind with target analytes, which results selective changes in the emission intensity [15]. The reports on the fluorescent-Cu2+ ion sensors revealed that Cu2+ ion sensing is either quenching or enhancing the fluorescence emission peak [15–17]. Among the fluorescent sensors, ultra-small CDs have emerged as a new class of nanomaterials and proven to be as alternative promising and eco-friendly fluorescent probes to organic and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [18]. Due to their surface passivations and presence of active organic groups, the CDs have been used as potential candidates in various applications including sensing [19], imaging study [20], drug delivery [21] and photocatalysis [22], respectively. Green synthetic protocols have been established for the synthesis of CDs using various natural sources as green precursors such as strawberry [23], corn flour [24], water melon peel [25], ginger [26] and coriander leaves [27]. In addition, simple green synthetic approaches have been developed for the fabrication of fluorescent CDs using Chinese coal [28] and petroleum coke [29] as carbon sources. Furthermore, microwave-assisted synthetic approaches have been shown to be the rapid, and cost-effective routes for the fabrication of ultra-small fluorescent CDs [30]. To explore the use of ultra-small fluorescent CDs in Cu2+ ion sensing, few research groups have been developed fluorescence detection methods for Cu2+ ion with wider detection range from 0 to 15 μM using CDs as probes. Briefly, Liu’s group synthesized polymer nanodots for sensing of Cu2+ ion [31]. Wang and co-workers fabricated graphene QDs with blue color emission for sensing of Cu2+ ion with detection limit of 0.226 μM [32]. Few groups have been reported the use of bamboo leaves [33], branched poly (ethylenimine) (BPEI) [34] and poly-ethylenimine (PEI) [35] for the fabrication of fluorescent CDs and their applications in Cu2+ ion sensing. Hu et al., synthesized fluorescent CDs with two different distinctive emissions from coal and used as a probe for sensing of Cu2+ ion [35]. Wang’s group illustrated the utility of petroleum coke for the synthesis of yellow color fluorescent CDs for label-free Cu2+ ion sensing [37]. Rong et al. explored the use of aminophenylboronic acid as carbon precursor for synthesis of heteroatom doped CDs for sensing of Cu2+ ion [38]. The fluorescent CDs were decorated on the surfaces of magnetic nanoparticles for the detection of Cu2+ ions and imaging of HeLa cells [39]. Recently, the fluorescent CDs from were fabricated from pyrolytic residue and used as a fluorescent probe for sensing of Cu2+ ion [40].

In the present work, we have fabricated a fluorescent Cu2+ ion sensor based on the use of ultra-small CDs derived from Acacia concinna seeds (shikakai) as a green precursor via microwave heating at 800 W for 2.0 min. The fabricated ultra-small CDs have strong ability to sense Cu2+ ion with high sensitivity and selectivity in the manner of fluorescence “turn-off” mechanism. This is due to the photo-induced electron transfer mechanism in the inner core of CDs, suggesting the formation of strong coordination Cu2+ ion-CDs complexes, which causes to quench the fluorescence of CDs via electron-hole recombination. Taking advantage of fluorescence off phenomenon, the fluorescent CDs acted as promising probes for assaying of Cu2+ ion various water samples. Furthermore, the fabricated CDs exhibited good biocompatible nature for multicolour imaging of Penicillium sp. cells. Scheme 1 illustrates the fabrication of ultra-small fluorescent CDs derived from shikakai and their Cu2+ ion sensing and cell imaging applications.

The Acacia concinna (shikakai) seeds were purchased from the local herbal shop, Surat, India. Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, Cr(NO3)2.6H2O, Hg(NO3)2·H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, NiSO4·6H2O, CaCl2·2H2O, AlCl3, Ba(NO3)2·4H2O were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Corp., USA. Acetone, methanol (MeOH) and acetonitrile (ACN) were purchased from Finar Ltd, India. Dialysis membrane-70 was purchased from HiMedia Labortories Pvt., India. Isoproturon and chlorpyrifos were received as gift samples from Gharda Chemicals Limited, India. Tebuconazole was procured from Atul Ltd, India. Dichlorovs and metalaxyl were received from United Phosphorus Limited, India. All experiments were performed by using Milli-Q-purified water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ultra-small CDs using shikakai
ce:section.ce:para: The ultra-small CDs were synthesized by using the microwave treatment of Acacia concinna (shikakai) seeds. Firstly, 3.0 g of shikakai was dissolved in MeOH and water at 1:1 ratio (20 mL), then the extract was filtered through 0.45 μm syringe filter. The obtained filtrate was carbonized by applying microwave heating at 800 W for 2 min. After the microwave reaction, the obtained carbon crude was again filtered through 0.45 μm syringe filter. To obtain the ultra-small CDs, the filtrate was dialysed (molecular cut off 12000–14000 Da) against Milli-Q-purified water for 9 h using dialysis membrane and then stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cu2+ ion using ultra-small CDs derived from shikakai
ce:section.ce:para: The optical characteristics and detection ability of ultra-small CDs to Cu2+ ion were investigated by fluorescence spectrometry. In brief, the appropriate concentrations of Cu2+ ion (0.7 mL, 0.01–500 μM) were added separately to 0.5 mL of CDs solutions and then vortexed for 10 min. The fluorescence responses of the above solutions were measured at excitation wavelength of 468 nm. The fluorescence behaviour of ultra-small CDs with other metal ions (Hg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+, 500 μM) was also investigated, confirming that no fluorescence quenching was observed.

Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics, USA) was used to record UV–vis absorption spectra of ultra-small CDs at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra of ultra-small CDs were measured by using Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured on a Shimadzu (FT-IR 8400S, Japan). To calculate the size and morphology of CDs, high resolution-transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained on JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan. Samsung microwave oven was (2450 MHz frequency and power at 800 W) used for the fabrication of ultra-small CDs (Samsung, India).

The quantum yield (Q) of ultra-small CDs was calculated by the following formula
Q
C D s
=
Q R
⋅
I
C D s
I R
⋅
A R
A
C D s
⋅
η
C D s
2
η R 2
Where ‘I’ represents the integrated fluorescent intensity, ‘A’ stands for the absorbance and ‘η’ is the refractive index of the solvent used; The subscripts ‘CD’ represents carbon dots and ‘R’ for reference are used in this equation.

The ultra-small fluorescent CDs were derived from Acacia concinna (shikakai) seeds as carbon precursor using microwave heating at 800 W for 2 min. The shikakai consists wide variety of phytochemicals including spinasterol, lupeol, lactone, acacic acid, tartric acid, succinic acid, citric acid, alkaloids, ascorbic acid and sugars (glucose, arabinose and rhamnose). These phytochemicals of shikakai play significant role in generating ultra-small fluorescent CDs with more organic functional groups that can make them as a selective probe for Cu2+ sensing. Before evaluating the analytical data, we have investigated the effect of various solvent medium (MeOH, ACN and acetone) for the extraction of phytochemicals from shikakai (Supporting Information of Fig. S1). The maximum fluorescence intensity of CDs was observed using 50% MeOH as a solvent medium, confirming that the extraction of more number of phytochemicals that allow to fabricate CDs with high degree of surface passivation. Thus, we selected 50% MeOH as the best solvent medium for the extraction of phytochemicals and fabrication of CDs with high surface passivation. In addition, the quantum yield (QY) of the shikakai CDs was estimated by using 50% MeOH, ACN, and acetone as solvents. The QY of shikakai CDs was found to be 10.20, 7.20, and 7.85% using 50% MeOH, ACN, and acetone as solvents.

To investigate the characteristic optical (absorption and fluorescence) properties of ultra-small CDs, the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of ultra-small CDs were measured and shown in Fig. 1 . The UV–vis absorption spectrum of ultra-small CDs exhibits maximum absorbance at 317 nm, signifying that the existence of π-π and n-π electronic transition in the CDs, which results efficient energy transfer in between excited π* of sp2 of ultra-small CDs and electron-rich organic groups [33–35]. In addition, the maximum emission peak was observed at λex/λem = 390/468 nm. The excitation wavelength-dependent emission feature of ultra-small CDs was investigated by studying the emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths i.e., 350 to 450 nm (Supporting Information of Fig. S2). With increasing excitation wavelength from 350 to 450 nm, the emission peak intensity at 468 nm was gradually increased and then largely red-shifted to 524 nm with decreasing intensity. These optical features revealed that the generation of ultra-small CDs with different size distribution, and the existence of emissive traps in the CDs [33–35], The fluorescence quantum yield of ultra-small CDs was 10.20%, which is superior than the reported methods [26,27]. The bright-blue color emission of ultra-small CDs can easily be observed with naked-eye when irradiated the solution under UV light at 365 nm (Inset of Fig. 1).

The organic functional groups on ultra-small CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Supporting Information of Fig. S3a). Typically, the ultra-small fluorescent CDs derived from shikakai exhibit characteristic peaks at 3600 − 3200 cm−1, which is ascribed to the stretching of OH and NH2 groups. The peak at 1325 cm−1 is attributed to CH stretching of aromatic rings. The peak at 1016 cm-1 is assigned to CN stretching of CDs. The peaks at 896 and 777 cm-1 belong to the CH bending of aromatic rings. The peaks at 3000 − 2800 and 1610 cm−1 correspond to the stretching of CH and CN groups. The peak at 1778 cm−1 was attributed due to the stretching of CO groups. These characteristic features confirm that the synthesis of CDs with electron-rich organic functional groups. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was studied for the estimation of size and morphology of ultra-small CDs derived from shikakai (Fig. 2 a). The as-synthesized ultra-small CDs are in spherical shape and well dispersed in aqueous medium with an average size of 2.5 ± 0.7 nm (Fig. 2b). The DLS data also confirm that an average diameter of CDs is <9.0 nm (Supporting Information of Fig. S3b), which includes the hydrodynamic radius of CDs. These analytical characteristics revealed that the as-synthesized fluorescent CDs are in ultra-small size with the existence of electron-rich organic functional groups on their surfaces, which can trap selectively the trace level metal ion. As shown in Supporting Information of Fig. S3c, the EDX data suggest that the percentages of carbon, and oxygen are 56.77 and 37.88%, revealing the presence of organic groups on the surfaces of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence “turn-off” detection of Cu2+ ion using ultra-small CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission properties of ultra-small CDs (0.5 mL) with various metal ions (Hg2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+ and Cu2+, 0.7 mL; 500 μM) were separately investigated by fluorescence spectrometry. The fluorescence spectrum of ultra-small CDs exhibits λex/λem = 390/468 nm, however, only Cu2+ ion remarkably quenches the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3 ) and a perceived fluorescence “turn-off” from bright blue to color-off. Meanwhile, the bright blue emission of ultra-small CDs was distinctly quenched with increasing concentration of Cu2+ ion (Inset of Fig. 3). These results indicated that the remarkable fluorescence quenching was observed upon only the addition of Cu2+ ion, suggesting that the ultra-small CDs act as a selective sensor for Cu2+ ion. Moreover, the quantum yield of the CDs was calculated with the addition of Cu2+ ion, and found to be 2.9%, using quinine sulphate as a reference material. Further, the selectivity of fluorescent CDs was investigated by measuring the emission spectra of CDs with the addition of various biomolecules and organic compounds. To this, various molecules (glucose, dopamine, carbidopa, cholesterol, urea, ascorbic acid, citric acid and fructose, 500 μM, 0.5 mL) were added separately into 1.0 mL of CDs solution and their emission spectra of measured (Supporting Information of Fig. S4). It was observed that none of these compounds make any impact on the emission intensity of CDs at 468 nm. To improve the sensing ability of shikakai CDs towards Cu2+ ion, the effect of pH was studied on the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of Cu2+ ion at phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) pH from 2.0 to 12.0 (Supporting Information of Fig. S5). These results reveal that the degree of fluorescence quenching of CDs ion is low with the addition of Cu2+ ion at PBS pH from 2.0 – 12.0. However, the high degree of fluorescence quenching of CDs is high in the presence of only Cu2+ ion without PBS pH, revealing the pH of the solution did not show any influence on the fluorescence quenching of CDs with the addition of Cu2+ ion. In view of this observation, the evaluation of Cu2+ ion was carried out without addition of buffer pH.

As per the spectroscopic and electron microscopic data, the as-synthesized ultra-small CDs consist various active functional groups, which have tendency to bind with target analytes via various mechanisms (electron transfer, inner filter effect, and aggregation induced emission quenching [33]. Based on these mechanisms, the surface organic groups (COOH and NH2) of ultra-small CDs have strong ability to bind selectively with Cu2+ ion, resulting to aggregate ultra-small CDs, which yields to quench the fluorescence intensity with high degree. Further, Cu2+ ion has shown high tendency to form complexation with the surface amino groups (NH2) of ultra-small CDs, leading to split d-orbital of Cu2+ ion, which allows electron transfer from excited state of CDs to d-orbital of Cu2+ ion [33]. As a result, the electron transition of CDs (radiation form) was completely squeezed that results the quenching of CDs fluorescence intensity. To confirm this, we have studied the HR-TEM image of ultra-small CDs with Cu2+ ion (Supporting Information of Fig. S6), confirming that the aggregation of CDs induced by Cu2+ ion, which is due to the formation of complex between Cu2+ ion and -NH2 groups of CDs. Therefore, the ultra-small fluorescent CDs senses Cu2+ ion principally based on the electron transfer and aggregation induced emission quenching mechanisms. As shown in Supporting Information of Fig. S7, the fluorescence lifetime data of CDs and CDs with Cu2+ ion were measured at excitation and emission wavelengths of 390 and 468 nm. The fluorescence lifetime of pure CDs was 6.4 ns, however, the life time of CDs was decreased to 5.1 ns, which is due to the addition of Cu2+ ion into CDs. This results suggested that the formation of complex at excited state, which leads to quench the fluorescence of CDs via dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of ultra-small CDs for detection of Cu2+ ion

ce:section.ce:para: To investigate sensing compatibility of ultra-small CDs towards Cu2+ ion in environmental conditions, the fluorescence emission spectra of ultra-small CDs were examined upon the addition of Cu2+ ion (500 μM) in the presence of various chemical species (metal ions - Hg2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Pb2+, Zn2+ and Fe3+; anions - SO4 2, S2−, PO4 3-, Cr2O7 2−, Cl−, Br−, I− and F−; and pesticides - tebuconazole, metalaxyl, chlorpropham, chlorpyrifos, isoproturon and dichlorovs, 500 μM) commonly present in environmental samples. Indeed, as depicted in Supporting Information of Fig. S8, the fluorescence emission intensity of ultra-small CDs was remarkably quenched only in the presence of Cu2+ ion, whereas no significant fluorescence quenching was noticed with other environmentally competing chemical species. These results revealed that ultra-small CDs acted as a Cu2+ ion selective sensor.

Due to their well dispersibility and ultra-small fluorescent nature, the CDs can be used ideal biocompatible probes for bioimaging applications. The cell culture and labelling procedure have already been well described in our previous reports [49,50]. The cultured Penicillium sp. fungal cells were treated with ultra-small CDs (10 μg/mL) and then fixed on glass slide using 4% paraformaldehyde solution in phosphate-buffered saline. As shown in Fig. 5 , the ultra-small CDs-incubated Penicillium sp. cells exhibited bright blue, green and red fluorescence colors under laser excitation wavelengths of 405, 488 and 561 nm, confirming that the ultra-small CDs were successfully internalized into the cells via endocytosis, and located in cytoplasm, and especially cocooned in the nucleus of cells. This result revealed that the ultra-small CDs derived from shikakai exhibit good biocompatibility and easily internalize into the cells with multi-color imaging, which signifies their potential application in cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of ultra-small CDs for assaying of Cu2+ ion in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, the practical application of ultra-small fluorescent CDs derived from shikakai CDs was assessed by detecting Cu2+ ion in spiked water samples (drinking, tap and river water) and the obtained results were depicted in Table 2 . It can be noticed that the method has shown satisfactory results with mean recovery ranges from 99.3 to 101.9% and the relative standard deviations (RSD%) were less than 1%. These results suggest that the ultra-small CDs derived from shikakai acted as potential candidates for assaying of Cu2+ ion in real samples.

In summary, one step green synthetic route was developed for the fabrication of ultra-small fluorescent CDs using shikakai as a carbon source. It displays strong emission peak at 468 nm when excited at 390 nm with the higher quantum yield (10.20%). The as-synthesized ultra-small CDs exhibited strong affinity and good selectivity and sensing ability for Cu2+ ion. The LOD of the method was found to be 4.3 nM, which is lower to the WHO recommendation for Cu2+ ion in drinking water. Importantly, the ultra-small CDs have shown high selectivity to detect Cu2+ ion over the other chemical species. Further, the ultra-small CDs were also demonstrated to be a non-toxic probe for multicolor imaging of fungal cells. Thus, the ultra-small CDs derived from shikakai can be used as promising probes for the development of simple and rapid analytical strategies for Cu2+ ion sensing and cell imaging applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support by Department of Science and Technology, Government of India (EMR/2016/002621/IPC). Ms. Bhamore acknowledges the Director, SVNIT, Surat for financial support under the Doctoral Program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.08.149.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

The Wilson disease gene is a putative copper transporting P–type ATPase similar to the Menkes gene

Chemometric determination of the botanical origin for Chinese honeys on the basis of mineral elements determined by ICP-MS

Remote stripping analysis of lead and copper by a mercury-coated platinum microelectrode

A new ratiometric lysosomal copper(II) fluorescent probe to map a dynamic metallome in live cells
Fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ with a tunable emission wavelength
Fluorescent carbon dots: rational synthesis, tunable optical properties and analytical applications

Water-soluble quantum dots for multiphoton fluorescence imaging in vivo
Fluorescent carbon dots derived from vancomycin for flutamide drug delivery and cell imaging
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
One-pot green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles as fluorescent probes for mercury ions
Dual functional carbon dots derived from cornflour via a simple one-pot hydrothermal route
Facile synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots using watermelon peel as a carbon source
Carbon dots prepared from ginger exhibiting efficient inhibition of human hepatocellular carcinoma cells
Green synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots from coriander leaves and their potential application as antioxidants, sensors and bioimaging agents
Organic amine-grafted carbon quantum dots with tailored surface and enhanced photoluminescence properties
Preparation of functionalized water-soluble photoluminescent carbon quantum dots from petroleum coke
Microwave synthesis of carbon dots with multi-response using denatured proteins as carbon source

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost, green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu (II) ions
Graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly efficient detection of copper (II) ions
One-step green synthesized fluorescent carbon nanodots from bamboo leaves for copper (II) ion detection
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescence probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Highly fluorescent carbon dots as selective and visual probes for sensing copper ions in living cells via an electron transfer process
Chemically tailoring coal to fluorescent carbon dots with tuned size and their capacity for Cu (II) detection
Yellow-visual fluorescent carbon quantum dots from petroleum coke for the efficient detection of Cu2+ ions

One-pot synthesis of carbon dot-entrenched chitosan-modified magnetic nanoparticles for fluorescence-based Cu2+ ion sensing and cell imaging
Synthesis of green fluorescent carbon quantum dots using waste polyolefins residue for Cu2+ ion sensing and live cell imaging

Exploring a new rapid colorimetric detection method of Cu2+ with high sensitivity and selectivity

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jigna Bhamore received M.Sc., in Chemistry from S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat in 2013. She is pursuing her Ph.D under the supervision of Dr. S.K. Kailasa. Her research interests include using metal nanoparticles as sensors for bioassays.

Sanjay Jha completed his Ph.D in Microbiology from M.S. University, Baroda, Gujarat. Currently, he is working as Associate Professor, Gujarat Agricultural Biotechnology Institute, Navsari Agricultural University, Surat, India. He is interested in nanomaterials interactions with cells and their confirmations by microscopic tools.

Tae Jung Park received BS and MS degree from the Chungnam National University, Korea, in 1998 and 2000. From December 1999 through May 2003, he had worked for Daewoong Pharmaceutical Company and Bioventure Company in Korea, where he developed a protein engineering in microbes as a researcher. And, he received PhD degree at the department of chemical and biomolecular engineering of KAIST in 2008. He is currently an adjunct professor at the BioProcess Engineering Research Center and vice director at Center for Nanobio Integration & Convergence Engineering, National Nanofab Center in KAIST. His research interests are novel platform technologies for the nanobio-fusion studies on the metal surface, biosensor chip and nanocomplex fabrication for electrochemical analysis as well as microfluidics. Also, he is currently interested in an investigation of miniaturization and an evaluation for the Biomimetics using nanomaterials. He received 8 Best Research Awards in the academic conferences. He has authored or co-authored over 70 papers, and holds 33 international patents as well as 33 Korean patents.

Suresh Kumar Kailasa completed his Ph. D. in Chemistry from S. V. University, Tirupati in 2005. After completing his two postdoctoral fellowships at South Korea and at National Sun Yat-Sen University, Taiwan he joined as a faculty at S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat in 2009. He received Young Scientist Award from Taiwan Mass Spectrometry Society in 2013. His interest includes nanomaterials integration in MALDI-MS and development of nanomaterials-based colorimetric sensors for biomolecules, drugs and metal ions.
